full_response:
1. Definition of a global inertial frame  
   – A global inertial frame is a coordinate system \((x^\mu)\) covering all spacetime such that  
     \[g_{\mu\nu}(x)=\eta_{\mu\nu}\quad\text{everywhere}\,,\]  
     and hence all Christoffel symbols vanish identically,  
     \[\Gamma^\rho_{\mu\nu}(x)=0\quad\forall x\,.\]

2. Gravity as spacetime curvature  
   – In general relativity, the presence of mass–energy produces curvature of spacetime, encoded in the Riemann tensor  
     \[R^\rho_{\ \sigma\mu\nu} 
        = \partial_\mu \Gamma^\rho_{\nu\sigma}
        - \partial_\nu \Gamma^\rho_{\mu\sigma}
        + \Gamma^\rho_{\mu\lambda}\,\Gamma^\lambda_{\nu\sigma}
        - \Gamma^\rho_{\nu\lambda}\,\Gamma^\lambda_{\mu\sigma}\,.\]  
   – If a spacetime admits a global inertial frame, then \(g_{\mu\nu}=\eta_{\mu\nu}\) everywhere ⇒ \(\Gamma^\rho_{\mu\nu}=0\) ⇒  
     \[R^\rho_{\ \sigma\mu\nu}=0\quad\text{everywhere.}\]

3. Physical fact: gravitational fields produce nonzero curvature  
   – In the vicinity of a mass \(M\), the Schwarzschild (or any nontrivial) solution has  
     \[R^\rho_{\ \sigma\mu\nu}\neq0\]  
     so tidal forces (geodesic deviation) cannot be transformed away globally.

4. Equivalence principle and locality  
   – The equivalence principle allows us to choose locally (in a small neighborhood of a point) coordinates so that  
     \[g_{\mu\nu}(p)=\eta_{\mu\nu},\quad \Gamma^\rho_{\mu\nu}(p)=0\,,\]  
     but the second derivatives of the metric (i.e.\ the Riemann tensor) remain nonzero: tidal effects persist.

5. Conclusion  
   – Because gravity manifests itself through spacetime curvature (nonzero Riemann tensor) which cannot be set to zero by any global coordinate transformation, one cannot find a single inertial frame covering an entire gravitational field.  

\boxed{Global inertial frames require a flat (zero‐curvature) spacetime, but gravity curves spacetime—tidal effects (nonzero Riemann tensor) cannot be eliminated by any global change of coordinates, so only local inertial frames exist in a gravitational field.}

Confidence: 90%
extracted_answer: Global inertial frames require a flat (zero‐curvature) spacetime, but gravity curves spacetime—tidal effects (nonzero Riemann tensor) cannot be eliminated by any global change of coordinates, so only local inertial frames exist in a gravitational field.
